explanation:
Bash is the default shell on Linux and macOS. Every command follows the pattern: command [options] [arguments]. The shell expands wildcards, redirects output, and pipes between commands.

The filesystem is a tree rooted at /. You navigate with cd, list with ls, and inspect with pwd. Understanding paths is fundamental.
example:
```bash
pwd
ls -la
cd /home/user
cd ..

touch newfile.txt
cp file1.txt file2.txt
mv file1.txt /tmp/
rm file1.txt
mkdir -p path/to/dir

ls *.txt
ls file?.txt

echo hello > file.txt
echo world >> file.txt
ls /nonexistent 2> errors.log

ls -la | grep ".txt" | wc -l
cat file.txt | sort | uniq
```
key_takeaway: Bash commands follow command options arguments. Pipes chain commands, redirections save output. Wildcards match filenames.
try_it: List all .json files sorted by size and save top 5 to a file.

explanation:
Bash variables are created by assignment (no spaces) and referenced with $. Strings can be single-quoted (literal) or double-quoted (variable expansion). Conditionals use if/elif/else with [[ ]].

Bash is weakly typed. Arithmetic uses $(( )). The exit status $? (0=success) drives conditional logic.
example:
```bash
NAME="Alice"
AGE=30
echo "Hello, $NAME!"
echo "Length: ${#NAME}"

echo "${NAME,,}"
echo "${NAME^^}"

A=10
B=3
SUM=$((A + B))

if [[ -z "$NAME" ]]; then
  echo "Name is empty"
elif [[ ${#NAME} -lt 3 ]]; then
  echo "Name too short"
else
  echo "Name is valid"
fi

if [[ -f "config.txt" ]]; then
  echo "File exists"
fi

grep -q "root" /etc/passwd
if [[ $? -eq 0 ]]; then
  echo "root user found"
fi
```
key_takeaway: Variables have no spaces in assignment. Use $(( )) for arithmetic, [[ ]] for tests. $? holds the exit status.
try_it: Write a script that checks if a file exists, is readable, and is non-empty.

explanation:
Bash has for loops (iterate over lists), while loops (repeat while condition), and until loops. Functions use positional parameters ($1, $2, $@).

Loops are essential for batch processing. Functions organize code into reusable blocks.
example:
```bash
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
  echo "Number: $i"
done

for file in *.txt; do
  echo "Processing $file"
  wc -l "$file"
done

for i in {1..10}; do
  echo $i
done

count=0
while [[ $count -lt 5 ]]; do
  echo "Count: $count"
  ((count++))
done

while IFS= read -r line; do
  echo "Line: $line"
done < input.txt

greet() {
  local name="$1"
  local greeting="${2:-Hello}"
  echo "$greeting, $name!"
  return 0
}

result=$(greet "Alice" "Hi")
echo "$result"
```
key_takeaway: For loops iterate over lists, while loops repeat on conditions. Functions use local variables and return via echo.
try_it: Write a function that counts .py files recursively in a directory.

explanation:
Bash has powerful text tools: grep (search patterns), sed (find/replace), awk (column processing), cut, sort, uniq, wc. These work together via pipes.

The philosophy is small tools doing one thing well, composed via pipes.
example:
```bash
grep "error" logfile.txt
grep -i "error" logfile.txt
grep -r "TODO" ./src/
grep -c "ERROR" logfile.txt
grep -v "DEBUG" logfile.txt

sed 's/old/new/g' file.txt
sed -i 's/old/new/g' file.txt
sed '3d' file.txt
sed -n '5,10p' file.txt

awk '{print $1, $3}' file.txt
awk -F: '{print $1}' /etc/passwd
awk '$3 > 100 {print $1}' data.txt
awk '{sum += $1} END {print sum}' numbers.txt

cut -d: -f1,3 /etc/passwd
sort -k2 -n data.txt
sort data.txt | uniq -c | sort -rn
wc -l *.txt

cat access.log | grep "GET /api" | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
```
key_takeaway: grep searches, sed edits, awk extracts columns. Chain them with pipes for powerful text processing.
try_it: Find top 10 IP addresses from an access.log using pipes.

explanation:
Production scripts need: shebang, set -euo pipefail, proper quoting, trap for cleanup. Debug with set -x.

Common pitfalls: unquoted variables, missing error handling. Shellcheck catches most bugs.
key_takeaway: Always use set -euo pipefail, quote all variables, trap for cleanup, and run shellcheck.
try_it: Write a robust backup script that validates args, creates timestamped tar.gz, and cleans up on failure.

practice_challenges.title: Log File Analyzer
practice_challenges.hint: Use grep to filter, awk to extract fields, sort | uniq -c for counting
practice_challenges.description: Write a bash script that parses an Apache access log and reports: top 10 IPs by request count, most requested URLs, and HTTP status code distribution.
practice_challenges.example_output: Top IPs table, top URLs table, status code percentages

practice_challenges.title: Backup Rotation Script
practice_challenges.hint: Use find with -mtime to locate old files, date to generate timestamps
practice_challenges.description: Write a script that creates daily backups of a directory, keeps the last 7 daily backups, the last 4 weekly backups, and deletes everything older. Log actions to a file.
practice_challenges.example_output: backup_2026-05-26.tar.gz created, 2 old backups deleted, log updated

practice_challenges.title: Multi-Server Health Monitor
practice_challenges.hint: Use ssh with -o ConnectTimeout, run checks in background with & and wait
practice_challenges.description: Write a script that checks disk space, memory, and CPU load on 10 remote servers via SSH in parallel, generates a formatted report, and sends an email alert if any server exceeds thresholds.
practice_challenges.example_input: servers.txt with 10 hostnames, thresholds: disk 80%, memory 90%
practice_challenges.example_output: Formatted table with all server metrics, email sent for 2 servers over threshold

practice_challenges.title: Interactive Menu System with Dialog
practice_challenges.hint: Use dialog --menu for navigation, case for routing, functions for each screen
practice_challenges.description: Build an interactive TUI menu for system administration tasks: view system info, manage services, check logs, manage users. Each menu item calls a function with error handling.
practice_challenges.example_input: User selects Manage Services > Restart Nginx
practice_challenges.example_output: Nginx restarted, success message displayed, returns to menu

practice_challenges.title: Automated Deployment Pipeline
practice_challenges.hint: Use trap for cleanup, functions for each stage, exit codes for flow control
practice_challenges.description: Write a deployment script that: pulls code from git, runs tests, builds artifacts, backs up current deployment, deploys new version, runs smoke tests, and rolls back automatically if any step fails. Include colored output and a deployment log.
practice_challenges.example_input: Deploy branch release/v2.1 to production server
practice_challenges.example_output: All 6 stages pass, deployment log written, Slack notification sent

explanation:
Bash manages processes: foreground, background (&), stopped (Ctrl+Z). Chain with && (success), || (failure), ; (always). Subshells and command substitution run in child processes.

Process management is critical for scripts: waiting for background tasks, handling signals, parallel execution.
example:
```bash
sleep 100 &
jobs
fg %1

mkdir -p /tmp/test && cd /tmp/test && echo "success"
false || echo "fallback"

for i in {1..5}; do
  sleep $i &
done
wait

find . -name "*.txt" | xargs -P 4 -I {} gzip {}

diff <(ls dir1) <(ls dir2)

trap 'echo "Caught SIGINT"; exit 1' INT
trap 'echo Cleaning up...; rm -f /tmp/lockfile' EXIT

timeout 5s long_running_command

sleep 60 &
PID=$!
echo "Background PID: $PID"
kill $PID 2>/dev/null || true
```
key_takeaway: Use & for background, wait for completion, trap for signals/cleanup, xargs -P for parallelism.
try_it: Write a script that downloads 5 URLs in parallel and reports success/failure.

explanation:
Bash scripting problems test your ability to compose Unix tools using pipes, redirects, and control flow. The most important patterns involve text processing with awk, sed, grep, and cut; file operations with find, xargs, and globbing; and process management with background jobs, signals, and traps. Bash is about gluing tools together, not writing complex algorithms.

The key to mastering Bash problems is understanding the Unix philosophy: each tool does one thing well. Chain simple commands with pipes (|), use xargs for parallel execution, and leverage process substitution (<()) for comparing command outputs. Know how to handle errors with set -e, trap for cleanup, and proper quoting to avoid word splitting.
example:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Count word frequencies in a file (like a bash 'Counter')
# Usage: ./wordcount.sh filename.txt

set -euo pipefail

if [ $# -ne 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <filename>" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Convert to lowercase, split words, sort, count
cat "$1" \
    | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' \
    | tr -cs '[:alpha:]' '\n' \
    | sort \
    | uniq -c \
    | sort -rn \
    | head -20

# Alternative: using awk for the entire pipeline
cat "$1" | awk '{
    for (i = 1; i <= NF; i++) {
        word = tolower($i)
        gsub(/[^a-z]/, "", word)
        if (word != "") freq[word]++
    }
}
END {
    for (w in freq) printf "%7d %s\n", freq[w], w
}' | sort -rn | head -20
```
key_takeaway: Chain simple Unix tools with pipes — each does one thing well. Master grep, awk, sed, sort, and xargs for any text processing task.
try_it: Write a script that finds the 10 largest files in a directory tree, sorted by size, using only command-line tools.

practice_challenges.title: Log Error Extractor
practice_challenges.hint: Use grep to filter ERROR lines, awk to extract timestamps, sort and uniq -c to count frequencies.
practice_challenges.description: Write a script that reads a log file, extracts all ERROR lines, counts unique error messages, and displays the top 5 most frequent errors with their counts.
practice_challenges.expected_output: A sorted list of error messages with frequency counts, e.g. '42 Connection timeout', '18 Permission denied'

practice_challenges.title: Directory Size Reporter
practice_challenges.hint: Use `du -sh` with `find` to get directory sizes, pipe through `sort -rh` for human-readable sorting.
practice_challenges.description: Write a script that lists all subdirectories in the current directory, sorted by total size (largest first), showing human-readable sizes.

practice_challenges.title: Parallel File Processor
practice_challenges.hint: Use `find` to locate files, `xargs -P N` for N parallel processes, and a processing command. Collect results with a trap for cleanup.
practice_challenges.description: Write a script that finds all .jpg files in a directory, resizes them to max 800px width using ImageMagick (or any tool), processing 4 files in parallel. Report success/failure counts.
practice_challenges.expected_output: Progress output showing each file processed, final summary: '23 succeeded, 2 failed'

practice_challenges.title: CSV Data Aggregator
practice_challenges.hint: Use awk with `-F,` for CSV parsing. Build associative arrays for grouping. Use `sort` and `join` to merge data from multiple files.
practice_challenges.description: Given two CSV files (orders.csv with order_id,user_id,amount and users.csv with user_id,name), join them and output total spending per user, sorted by amount descending.

practice_challenges.title: System Health Monitor
practice_challenges.hint: Use trap for cleanup on SIGINT. Read /proc for CPU and memory. Use awk for threshold checks. Implement a simple alerting mechanism with cooldown.
practice_challenges.description: Write a daemon script that monitors CPU, memory, and disk usage every 30 seconds. Log warnings when thresholds are exceeded (CPU>80%, Mem>90%, Disk>95%). Support graceful shutdown via SIGTERM. Rotate logs when they exceed 10MB.
practice_challenges.expected_output: Log entries with timestamps showing system metrics and any threshold violations. Clean shutdown message on SIGTERM.
